1: 'Then he showed me Joshua the high priest standing before the angel of YHWH, and Satan standing at his right hand to accuse him.' The fourth night vision: the heavenly courtroom. Joshua (Yehoshua) the high priest — the same Joshua who co-leads the rebuilding with Zerubbabel — stands before the divine court. Satan (hasatan, the adversary/accuser) stands at his right hand — the position of the prosecuting attorney in the ancient Near Eastern legal court (Psalms 109:6). The accusation is unstated but the filthy garments (Zechariah 3:3) indicate it relates to the impurity of the post-exilic community.
2: 'And YHWH said to Satan, YHWH rebuke you, O Satan! YHWH who has chosen Jerusalem rebuke you! Is not this a brand plucked from the fire?' The divine rebuke of the accuser: YHWH dismisses the prosecution. The election of Jerusalem is YHWH's answer to the accusation — he has chosen this community despite its impurity. The brand plucked from the fire (ud mutsal me'esh) — a firebrand rescued from burning — is a vivid image of the exile community: nearly destroyed, barely saved, still smoking from the fire of judgment.
3: 'Now Joshua was standing before the angel, clothed with filthy garments.' The filthy garments (begadim tzoe'im) represent the impurity of the post-exilic community — its accumulated guilt, its mixed marriages (Ezra 9-10), its half-hearted observance, its defilements during the exile. The high priest's garments are supposed to be of pristine white linen (Exodus 28:39-43; Leviticus 16:4, 23-24); these filthy garments are the visual evidence of the accusation the adversary has brought.
4: 'And the angel said to those who were standing before him, Remove the filthy garments from him. And to him he said, Behold, I have taken your iniquity away from you, and I will clothe you with pure vestments.' The divine action of removing guilt: the iniquity (avon) is taken away not by Joshua's merit but by divine fiat. The pure vestments (machalatzot, festive/clean garments) replace the filthy ones. This is justification by divine action — the heavenly courtroom pronounces the accused clean, clothes him in righteousness. Paul's vision of Christ's imputed righteousness (Romans 5:17-19; 2 Corinthians 5:21) has its OT template here.
5: 'And I said, Let them put a clean turban on his head. So they put a clean turban on his head and clothed him with garments. And the angel of YHWH was standing by.' The prophet actively participates in the vision — Zechariah requests the turban. The clean turban (tzanif tahor) is the high-priestly head-covering inscribed 'Holy to YHWH' (Exodus 28:36-37; Zechariah 39:30). The complete high-priestly vestments restored: Joshua is re-consecrated, re-authorized, fit again for holy service.
6: 'And the angel of YHWH solemnly assured Joshua, saying, Thus says YHWH of hosts: If you will walk in my ways and keep my charge, then you shall rule my house and have charge of my courts, and I will give you the right of access among those who are standing here.' The conditional promise to Joshua: covenant faithfulness results in authority over YHWH's house (the temple) and access to the heavenly council (standing among the angels). The high priest's authority is not automatic but contingent on covenant walking — derakhim (ways) and mishmarti (charge/ordinances).
7: Verse 7 completes the conditional: if Joshua walks in YHWH's ways, he will rule the house and have access to the divine council. This is the rare explicit statement that the high priest mediates between the earthly and heavenly courts — his authority flows through both the temple below and the council above.
8: 'Hear now, O Joshua the high priest, you and your friends who sit before you, for they are men who are a sign: behold, I will bring my servant the Branch.' The sudden shift from Joshua's restoration to the messianic oracle: Joshua and his priestly companions are anshei mofet (men of sign/portent) — they symbolize something beyond themselves. The Branch (Tzamach) is introduced: the messianic title used in Jeremiah 23:5 ('I will raise up for David a righteous Branch') and Jeremiah 33:15. Isaiah 4:2 uses a related image (the Branch of YHWH). The Branch designates the coming Davidic ruler who will fulfill all covenant expectations.
9: 'For behold, on the stone that I have set before Joshua, on a single stone with seven eyes, I will engrave its inscription, declares YHWH of hosts, and I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day.' The stone with seven eyes — a foundation stone for the temple, or possibly the capstone (cf. Zechariah 4:7). The seven eyes represent comprehensive divine oversight (cf. 4:10 where the seven lamps are YHWH's eyes scanning the whole earth). The removal of iniquity in a single day (beyom echad) is the eschatological cleansing — not gradual moral improvement but decisive divine act. This anticipates the NT's understanding of the cross as the decisive, once-for-all atonement (Hebrews 9:12, 26; Zechariah 10:10).
10: 'In that day, declares YHWH of hosts, every one of you will invite his neighbor to come under his vine and under his fig tree.' The vine-and-fig-tree peace (Micah 4:4; 1 Kings 4:25; Zephaniah 3:13) — the image of complete safety, abundant prosperity, and communal harmony. Each man hosting his neighbor under his own vine and fig tree is the picture of a society where land theft (Micah 2:1-2) has been reversed and each family restored to its ancestral portion.
